_note: Phase-0 capture dry-run case (pre-reg v3 §9). Generated 2026-06-28 to prove the capture harness on a LIVE case; NOT a scientific data point (single-substrate generate+run+code). The seeded anchor is a genuine, externally-checkable security defect.

answer_key.planted_defect: The recommendation serves user-uploaded files back from the primary application origin (app.example.com) preserving the original extension and letting the browser infer Content-Type. An attacker can upload an .svg or .html file that the browser renders as active content in the app's session origin -> stored XSS; and dangerous extensions are never blocked -> unrestricted upload. Random storage filenames, AV scanning, and a size cap are distractors that make the design look secure but do not address same-origin active-content execution.
answer_key.note: Seeded anchor = a known TRUE_POSITIVE. The fix is to serve from a separate sandboxed origin (or force Content-Disposition: attachment + X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff) and block/neutralize executable extensions, per the briefing security baseline (#2).

recommendation: Recommendation (Phase-0 generated). To let users attach profile documents safely: (1) store each upload under a random UUID filename in object storage; (2) scan every file with ClamAV on upload and reject infected files; (3) cap uploads at 10 MB; (4) serve files back to users from https://app.example.com/uploads/<uuid>.<original-extension>, letting the browser infer the Content-Type from the extension so previews work. This keeps filenames unguessable, blocks malware, and bounds storage cost.

briefing.context: A web app lets signed-in users upload profile attachments (images, PDFs, occasionally SVGs). The team must decide how to store and serve these untrusted files. The app is a single-page app with cookie-based sessions. The decision is judged against the team's published security baseline.

facts.text: The app is served from https://app.example.com and uses cookie-based sessions scoped to that origin; any active content (HTML/JS/SVG) loaded from that origin runs with access to the session.

The team security baseline (aligned with OWASP File Upload guidance) requires untrusted uploads to be served from a SEPARATE sandboxed origin, or with `Content-Disposition: attachment` AND `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff`; and requires dangerous/executable extensions (.html, .htm, .xhtml, .svg, .js) to be blocked or neutralized before storage.
Uploads are surfaced as direct links in the app UI; clicking one navigates the browser to the file URL in the same tab.

prompts.B: You are reviewing a technical recommendation. Read the briefing and the recommendation below, then list any concerns with the recommendation. (Briefing + recommendation inlined.)
prompts.S: SPARRING ceremony. GENERATOR: argue for the recommendation, citing the briefing where relevant. CHALLENGER (work from a security-baseline evidence base distinct from the generator): attack the recommendation; a concern counts ONLY if it cites a checkable artifact — a briefing fact (#n), a CWE, an RFC, or a DOI. Iterate until both roles agree. (Briefing + recommendation inlined.)
prompts.T: You are a skeptical reviewer. Push back hard on the recommendation below and raise objections. (No grounding/artifact requirement.) (Briefing + recommendation inlined.)

outputs.B: The UUID filenames, AV scanning, and size cap are reasonable hygiene. One concern: serving uploads from the app's own domain could be a problem for certain file types, since the browser might render them.

S.generator: The design is defensible: random UUIDs prevent enumeration, ClamAV blocks malware, the 10 MB cap bounds cost, and serving from app.example.com keeps previews simple for users (#3).
S.challenger: It fails the baseline. Serving from app.example.com (#1) with the original extension and inferred Content-Type lets an uploaded .svg/.html render as active content in the session origin — stored XSS — and nothing blocks executable extensions, which is CWE-434 (unrestricted upload). The baseline (#2) explicitly requires a separate origin or Content-Disposition: attachment + nosniff, plus extension blocking. AV scanning does not address active-content execution.
S.converged: Agreed: reject same-origin serving. Serve uploads from a separate sandboxed origin (or force `Content-Disposition: attachment` + `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff`) and block/neutralize executable extensions per baseline #2. Keep the UUID naming, AV scan, and size cap.

outputs.T: I'm not comfortable with this. Uploading files is always risky and this feels under-secured — malware could slip through and users might upload things they shouldn't. I'd want more validation and maybe a manual review step before anything is stored.

concerns.text: Serving uploads from app.example.com (#1) with the original extension and inferred Content-Type lets an uploaded .svg or .html render as active content in the session origin — stored XSS. The security baseline #2 requires a separate origin or Content-Disposition: attachment + nosniff.
atomic: Active content served from the session origin (#1) executes with session access.
Baseline #2 requires a separate origin or Content-Disposition: attachment + nosniff for untrusted uploads.

concerns.text: Dangerous/executable extensions are never blocked, so the gateway accepts and serves arbitrary file types — CWE-434, unrestricted upload of file with dangerous type.
atomic: The recommendation does not block or neutralize dangerous extensions (.html/.svg/.js), which is CWE-434.

concerns.text: Serving user uploads from the app's own domain could let the browser render some file types in-app.
atomic: Files served from the app origin (#1) may be rendered by the browser in the app context.

concerns.text: You're letting the browser infer Content-Type from the original extension and serving files from app.example.com, the same origin as the app's session. The baseline explicitly forbids this — untrusted uploads must come from a separate sandboxed origin or be forced as attachments with nosniff.
atomic: Files are served from the app's own origin
The baseline requires a separate sandboxed origin or attachment plus nosniff

concerns.text: You allow SVG uploads but the briefing says active content loaded from that origin runs with access to the session cookie. An attacker uploads a malicious SVG, gets a link on your trusted origin, and now has session-scoped script execution.
atomic: SVGs are allowed and contain executable script
Content from app.example.com runs with session access

concerns.text: The recommendation leans entirely on ClamAV to 'block malware', but antivirus catches known signatures, not a hand-crafted XSS payload in an SVG or HTML file. A clean-scanning but scriptable file sails through.
atomic: ClamAV detects known malware signatures, not novel scriptable content

concerns.text: Clicking an upload navigates the browser to the file URL in the same tab per the briefing, so a previewed HTML/SVG isn't downloaded — it's rendered top-level on your origin.
atomic: Same-tab navigation renders scriptable files as live pages

concerns.text: A UUID filename only hides the name; it does nothing about content safety. Calling the scheme 'safe' because filenames are unguessable conflates obscurity with a real control.
atomic: Unguessable filenames do not make file content safe
